method: psql -f, wall-clock `time`, 15 iterations after 3-iter warmup. psql process startup + TLS handshake + auth is a fixed cost per invocation (proxied by a `select 1` baseline) and is subtracted out when attributing latency to wire work.

database.notes: Benchmark workload uses pooled Supabase as the high-latency remote target. The cyclic-schema integration test runs against a local Postgres 17 instance (see cycle_breaking.integration_test).

binary.notes: tsup minify=true, sourcemap=false. Sourcemaps were never consumed by end users (CLI ships as a shebang script) so removing them is a pure win for npm tarball size.

cycle_breaking.algorithm: After Kahn's topo sort fails, scan the residual edges for an FK column marked nullable. Remove it from the DAG (recorded as a BrokenEdge), retry Kahn's. Repeat until acyclic or no nullable back-edge remains. At runtime the child rows are inserted with NULL on the broken column, and a post-insert UPDATE pass — inside the same transaction, after the parent table is fully seeded — wires each child to a random parent PK. SET CONSTRAINTS ALL DEFERRED is issued before inserts so any DEFERRABLE constraints in the user's schema validate at COMMIT.

integration_test.fixture: two-table cycle: users(primary_org_id -> orgs) and orgs(owner_user_id -> users NOT NULL); users.primary_org_id is nullable, so it is the broken edge.
integration_test.result: 10 rows inserted across 2 tables; 0/5 NULL FKs on users.primary_org_id after the wire-up pass; both directions of the cycle reference valid parents.

cycle_breaking.limitations: NOT NULL cycle FKs remain unsupported. The CLI fails with an actionable message naming the cycle and suggesting either making the column nullable or excluding one of the cycle members.
